message.content: Pre-OS Boot mechanisms can be exploited by adversaries to establish persistence on a system by manipulating firmware and startup services that load before the operating system. This includes overwriting data in boot drivers or firmware such as BIOS and UEFI. Such attacks are challenging to detect because malware at this level operates below the operating system, evading host software-based defenses. The potential impact is significant, as it allows adversaries to maintain control over a system even after reboots and reinstallation of the operating system. Common exploitation methods include modifying firmware to introduce malicious code that executes during the boot process. Mitigation strategies involve ensuring firmware is up-to-date, enabling secure boot features, and using hardware-based security measures like Trusted Platform Modules (TPMs) to verify the integrity of the boot process. Regularly monitoring and auditing firmware for unauthorized changes is also crucial.
